questions.question: Why does a pure push fanout strategy break down for celebrity accounts?
options: Because celebrity accounts post too infrequently to keep feeds fresh.
Because a single post triggers as many writes as the account has followers, creating catastrophic write amplification.
Because Memcached cannot store more than 10K items per key.
Because Kafka topics cannot handle more than 1M messages per second.
questions.explanation: A celebrity with 100M followers causes 100M Memcached writes per post. Pure push collapses under this write amplification.

questions.question: What is the core trade-off between push fanout and pull-on-read for news feeds?
options: Push is cheap to read but expensive to write; pull is cheap to write but expensive to read.
Push always produces better ranking quality than pull.
Pull requires more storage than push because each post is copied to every follower.
Push is only suitable for accounts with fewer than 100 followers.
questions.explanation: Push precomputes feeds (expensive writes, cheap reads). Pull computes at read time (cheap writes, expensive reads). Hybrid routing balances both.

questions.question: Facebook reportedly uses approximately what follower count as the threshold to switch from push to pull fanout?
options: 1,000 followers
10,000 followers
50,000 followers
1,000,000 followers
questions.explanation: Facebook uses roughly 50K followers as the push/pull crossover. Below this threshold, push fanout is efficient; above it, the write amplification becomes unacceptable.

questions.question: When a dormant user (offline for 30 days) reactivates, what happens to their Memcached feed in a hybrid push system?
options: Their feed is fully populated from the fanout workers immediately upon login.
Their feed entry was likely evicted or never written because offline users are skipped during fanout; a cold-build from the post store is triggered.
They receive only celebrity posts because normal-user posts are never stored.
Their feed is frozen at the state it was in when they went offline.
questions.explanation: Hybrid systems skip offline users during fanout to save writes. On reactivation, the missing feed must be cold-built by querying followed accounts directly.

questions.question: A celebrity post goes viral and your Memcached cluster is evicting entries under the write pressure. What is the safest immediate mitigation?
options: Switch all celebrity posts to push fanout temporarily to fill MC caches.
Serve celebrity posts directly from the celebrity post store on MC miss, bypassing the cache write path.
Pause feed reads for all users until the viral storm subsides.
Increase Kafka partition count to reduce per-partition fanout load.
questions.explanation: When MC is under pressure, the read path can fall back to the celebrity post store directly. This avoids compounding the problem by adding more MC writes.

questions.question: Your ML ranking model starts returning a score of 0 for all posts. What should the feed service do?
options: Return an empty feed to the user.
Block feed reads until the ranking model recovers.
Fall back to a recency-sorted feed using post timestamps.
Only show posts from celebrity accounts since those have a separate score.
questions.explanation: Graceful degradation to chronological order ensures users still see content. A feed with recency sorting is far better than an error or empty state.

questions.question: Why does the hybrid fanout system skip offline users when writing to Memcached feeds?
options: Memcached does not support writes for keys associated with inactive sessions.
Writing to feeds of offline users wastes Memcached capacity and fanout worker throughput, with no immediate benefit since those users will not read the feed.
Offline users have a different Memcached cluster that requires a separate client.
It is a GDPR requirement to not store data for inactive users.
questions.explanation: Skipping offline users cuts fanout volume by 30–50% while wasting no user experience — dormant users will get a cold-built feed on their next login anyway.

questions.question: A user requests GDPR deletion. How should the system propagate the removal from all materialized feed caches?
options: Delete only the user's own post store; Memcached entries expire naturally via TTL.
Issue an immediate tombstone that blocks the deleted content at read time, then run an async job to remove it from all MC feed entries and backing stores.
Pause all fanout workers until the deletion is complete to prevent new copies from being created.
Remove the user from the TAO graph only; feed workers will automatically stop referencing deleted content.
questions.explanation: A tombstone provides immediate suppression at read time (within seconds). Async cleanup then removes the data from materialized caches and stores within the 72-hour GDPR window.
